access.guest_fallback_message: Detailed explanations are available for logged-in Saola students only.

q17.explanation: Câu 17 khớp với thông tin ở đoạn 1. Ý chính trong bài là: "Skye is truly a landscape fashioned by geology. Renowned for its ancient formations, this rugged island is home to some of the oldest rocks on Earth. The sheer variety of life demonstrated by these fossils can be explained by the different climatic conditions which Scotland underwent as a result of the movement of huge tectonic plates, causing this country to drift northwards from an original position near the South Pole to the equator, and beyond.". Vì câu hỏi diễn đạt lại cùng một ý, đáp án là TRUE.
q17.evidence: Skye is truly a landscape fashioned by geology. Renowned for its ancient formations, this rugged island is home to some of the oldest rocks on Earth. The sheer variety of life demonstrated by these fossils can be explained by the different climatic conditions which Scotland underwent as a result of the movement of huge tectonic plates, causing this country to drift northwards from an original position near the South Pole to the equator, and beyond._

q18.explanation: Câu 18 khớp với thông tin ở đoạn 2. Ý chính trong bài là: "These were forced to the surface about 1.1 billion years ago before being buried by several thousand metres of gritty and pebbly sandstones from fast-flowing rivers. Looking down upon the island from above, large extinct volcanoes - now much eroded - can be seen forcing their way through the centre of this ancient mass, forming the island’s lofty Cuillin hills. From their famous jagged peaks, peregrines and golden eagles watch over the lands below.". Vì câu hỏi diễn đạt lại cùng một ý, đáp án là TRUE.
q18.evidence: These were forced to the surface about 1.1 billion years ago before being buried by several thousand metres of gritty and pebbly sandstones from fast-flowing rivers. Looking down upon the island from above, large extinct volcanoes - now much eroded - can be seen forcing their way through the centre of this ancient mass, forming the island’s lofty Cuillin hills. From their famous jagged peaks, peregrines and golden eagles watch over the lands below._

q19.explanation: Câu 19 mâu thuẫn với thông tin ở đoạn 4. Bài nêu: "Above these, the high columnal cliffs provide nesting spots and lookouts for white-tailed sea eagles which take fish and the aforementioned small mammals directly from the sea. Below these cliffs, Skye’s numerous grey and common seals can be spotted entering and exiting the sea from their bases on the many flat islets around the southern and western coast, which mirror a geological feature of particular note known as ‘MacLeod’s tables’ - flat-topped hills formed by the erosion of paleocene lavas.". Câu hỏi đã đổi một chi tiết quan trọng hoặc đảo ngược ý trong bài, nên đáp án là FALSE.
q19.evidence: Above these, the high columnal cliffs provide nesting spots and lookouts for white-tailed sea eagles which take fish and the aforementioned small mammals directly from the sea. Below these cliffs, Skye’s numerous grey and common seals can be spotted entering and exiting the sea from their bases on the many flat islets around the southern and western coast, which mirror a geological feature of particular note known as ‘MacLeod’s tables’ - flat-topped hills formed by the erosion of paleocene lavas._

q20.explanation: Câu 20 khớp với thông tin ở đoạn 5. Ý chính trong bài là: "Further out towards this oceanic expanse, a wealth of species take advantage of the nutrient-rich waters typical of the Sea of the Hebrides. The first benefactors are plankton, which bloom each Spring and attract a host of predators including forage fish, such as herring. The most renowned visitor to the island’s waters, the colossal basking shark, also feasts during these springtime occurrences and can often be spotted from island viewpoints doing so.". Vì câu hỏi diễn đạt lại cùng một ý, đáp án là TRUE.
q20.evidence: Further out towards this oceanic expanse, a wealth of species take advantage of the nutrient-rich waters typical of the Sea of the Hebrides. The first benefactors are plankton, which bloom each Spring and attract a host of predators including forage fish, such as herring. The most renowned visitor to the island’s waters, the colossal basking shark, also feasts during these springtime occurrences and can often be spotted from island viewpoints doing so._
